---
title: "Florida Keys Hempvine (Mikania cordifolia)"
slug: "mikania-cordifolia"
group: "Plantae"
image: "https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/623204086/medium.jpg"        # keep for thumbnails
image-alt: "Florida Keys Hempvine"  # keep for a11y
tax_kingdom: "Plantae"
tax_class: "Magnoliopsida"
tax_order: "Ericales"
tax_family: "Primulaceae"
categories: ["Plantae", "Magnoliopsida", "Ericales", "Primulaceae"]   # these drive the chips + filtering
freeze: true
---

<figure class="species-figure">

<img src="https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/623204086/medium.jpg" alt="Florida Keys Hempvine" class="species-photo"/>

<figcaption class="photo-credit">

Photo: <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/people/anabelzou">
anabelzou</a> (CC BY-NC) via <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/342244342">iNaturalist</a>

</figcaption>

</figure>

## Taxonomy

-   **Class:** Magnoliopsida
-   **Order:** Ericales
-   **Family:** Primulaceae

## Occurrence at DeLuca Preserve

-   [DeLuca Bio Blitz Species Observations](https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?project_id=191727&subview=map&taxon_id=131169)

## Conservation & rarity context

-   IUCN: Not Listed
-   **Rarity note:** Rare species in Florida found in moist, to wet habitats such as marsh edges, coastal hammocks, and disturbed areas.

## Natural history notes

A twining vine characterized by its heart-shaped leaves and clusters of small white to pale pink flowers. In Florida, it is found in moist to wet habitats such as marsh edges, coastal hammocks, and disturbed areas, particularly in southern regions. It grows during the warmer months and often climbs over surrounding vegetation, providing nectar for a variety of pollinators.

## References / links

-   [iNaturalist species page](https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/204353-Lysimachia-minima)
-   [Local profile](https://florida.plantatlas.usf.edu/genus/788)
